rules.message: Hardcoded secret-shaped literal (an API-key/password/token assignment, or a known cloud-key prefix) â€” a credential committed to source is exposed to every reader/fork/clone and cannot be rotated without a code change. Move it to an environment variable or a secrets manager (e.g. `process.env.API_KEY`). Suppress a vetted case with `// secret-ok`.

`req.body` (or a spread of it) passed directly into a database write (`create`/`update`/`upsert`/`updateMany`) â€” an attacker can set any field the model accepts (role, isAdmin, price, ownerId, ...) that the handler never intended to expose. Whitelist the fields you accept, or use a validated DTO. Suppress a vetted case with `// mass-assignment-ok`.

`$queryRawUnsafe`/`$executeRawUnsafe` called â€” these run the given SQL as-is with no parameterization, so any request-derived string interpolated into it is a SQL injection. Use `$queryRaw`/`$executeRaw` tagged templates (or pass values as separate arguments) so Prisma parameterizes the query. Suppress a vetted case with `// raw-sql-ok`.

A cookie is set (`res.cookie`/`setCookie`/`cookies.set`) without `httpOnly` in the same function â€” a session/auth cookie without it is readable by any script on the page, so an XSS bug becomes full session theft. Add `{ httpOnly: true }` (plus `secure`/`sameSite` as appropriate). Suppress a vetted case with `// cookie-ok`.

CORS origin set to `*` (a wildcard `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` header, or `origin: '*'`) â€” lets any website make cross-origin requests to this endpoint, defeating same-origin protection. Return a specific allow-listed origin instead, especially on endpoints that accept cookies or auth headers. Suppress a vetted case with `// cors-ok`.

Password hashed/compared with a broken algorithm (MD5/SHA-1) or bcrypt at a single-digit cost factor â€” both are too cheap to resist offline brute force at billions of guesses/sec on commodity GPUs. Use bcrypt/scrypt/argon2 with a cost factor of at least 10-12. Suppress a vetted case with `// weak-hash-ok`.

A secret-shaped query parameter (`api_key`/`access_token`/`token`/`secret`) appears in a URL â€” URLs are logged by proxies, load balancers, browser history and the `Referer` header. Move the credential into an `Authorization` header (or body/cookie) instead. Suppress a vetted case with `// url-key-ok`.

JPA `@Query` built via string concatenation (`@Query("..." + ...)`) â€” the concatenated part is attacker-controlled input spliced into SQL/JPQL, the same SQL injection risk as building any query string by hand. Use named parameters (`:param` with `@Param`) or a parameterized query instead. Suppress a vetted case with `// query-concat-ok`.

`redirect(...)` is called in a function that also reads `req.query`/`req.params`/`req.body` â€” an unchecked request-derived target lets an attacker send victims to an arbitrary external site (open redirect), a common phishing/OAuth-token-theft vector. Validate the target against an allow-list of known internal paths. Suppress a vetted case with `// redirect-ok`.

An outbound HTTP call (`fetch`/`axios`/`got`) is made in a function that also reads `req.query`/`req.params`/`req.body` â€” if that value becomes part of the URL, the server can be made to request attacker-chosen hosts (SSRF), reaching internal services or a metadata endpoint. Validate the target against an allow-list of hosts. Suppress a vetted case with `// ssrf-ok`.

A filesystem call (`fs`/`fsp.*`, `readFile`/`writeFile`) reads a `path.join(...)` path derived from request input â€” an unvalidated `..` segment lets an attacker escape the intended directory (path traversal). Reject `..` segments, or verify the resolved path stays under the base directory. Suppress a vetted case with `// traversal-ok`.

`credentials: true` appears in a file that also configures `origin: '*'` â€” a wildcard origin with credentialed CORS lets any website make authenticated cross-origin requests carrying the victim's cookies/auth headers. Echo a specific allow-listed origin (never `*`) whenever `credentials: true` is set. Suppress a vetted case with `// cors-cred-ok`.

`jwt.sign(...)` is called where `expiresIn` never appears in the same function â€” a token with no expiry is valid forever, so a leak (logs, XSS, a compromised client) can be replayed indefinitely. Pass `{ expiresIn: '...' }` (or set `exp` in the payload) so tokens expire. Suppress a vetted case with `// jwt-expiry-ok`.

`Math.random()` is used on the same line as a token/otp/nonce/session-id/secret-shaped identifier â€” it's a predictable PRNG, not cryptographically secure, so a value generated this way can be guessed or brute-forced. Use `crypto.randomBytes(n).toString('hex')` or `crypto.randomUUID()`. Suppress a vetted case with `// weak-random-ok`.

A secret/token/signature/hmac/api-key-shaped identifier is compared with `===`/`!==` â€” equality operators short-circuit on the first differing byte, leaking a timing side-channel an attacker can exploit to recover the value byte-by-byte. Use `crypto.timingSafeEqual(...)` on fixed-length buffers instead. Suppress a vetted case with `// timing-ok`.

A raw error object is sent directly to the client (`res.status(5xx).send/json(err)`, or Hono's `c.json(err)`) â€” errors often carry stack traces, file paths, or internal config that help an attacker map the system. Log server-side and send a generic message (e.g. `{ error: 'Internal server error' }`) instead. Suppress a vetted case with `// error-leak-ok`.

A server-only-shaped env var (`SECRET`/`PRIVATE`/`SERVICE_ROLE`/`SERVICE_KEY`) is referenced from frontend code â€” build tools inline `process.env.*`/`import.meta.env.*` into the shipped JS bundle, making it readable in devtools. Move it behind a backend endpoint, or use a non-secret public var (`NEXT_PUBLIC_`/`VITE_`-prefixed). Suppress a vetted case with `// fe-env-ok`.

A token/JWT-shaped value is written to `localStorage` â€” readable by any script on the page, so a single XSS bug anywhere on the origin can exfiltrate it with no further exploitation. Store the token in an `httpOnly` cookie instead, so client-side JS (and XSS) cannot read it. Suppress a vetted case with `// ls-token-ok`.

A password-shaped literal is hardcoded â€” a direct assignment (`password = "..."`) or a JDBC `getConnection(url, user, password)` call with literal credentials â€” exposed to every reader of the repo and impossible to rotate without a code change. Read it from an environment variable, an excluded properties file, or a secrets manager. Suppress a vetted case with `// java-pwd-ok`.

`DocumentBuilderFactory`/`SAXParserFactory.newInstance()` with no XXE guard in the same method â€” a default-configured XML parser resolves external entities, so crafted XML can read local files, perform SSRF, or exhaust memory. Set `disallow-doctype-decl` or `FEATURE_SECURE_PROCESSING` before parsing untrusted input. Suppress a vetted case with `// xxe-ok`.

`ObjectInputStream.readObject()` is called in this function â€” Java's native deserialization invokes arbitrary code paths (gadget chains), so deserializing an attacker-controlled byte stream can lead to remote code execution. Use JSON/Protobuf instead, or validate the stream with an allow-list `ObjectInputFilter`. Suppress a vetted case with `// deser-ok`.

`new File(...)` is constructed in a function that also reads `request.getParameter(...)` â€” an unvalidated parameter flowing into the path lets an attacker escape the intended directory and read/write files outside it. Reject `..` segments, or resolve and compare against the base directory before use. Suppress a vetted case with `// java-traversal-ok`.

`new Random()` is used on the same line as a token/session/otp/nonce-shaped identifier â€” `java.util.Random` is a predictable PRNG, not cryptographically secure, so a value generated this way can be predicted or brute-forced. Use `java.security.SecureRandom` instead. Suppress a vetted case with `// java-random-ok`.

An exception's stack trace/message (`printStackTrace()`/`.getMessage()`) touches the HTTP response (`getWriter()`, `ResponseEntity`, `@ResponseBody`) in the same method â€” if it reaches the client, it exposes internal class names and file paths. Log server-side and return a generic error body instead. Suppress a vetted case with `// stacktrace-ok`.

TLS certificate/hostname verification is disabled (a trust-all `X509TrustManager`, `ALLOW_ALL_HOSTNAME_VERIFIER`, or a `setHostnameVerifier` lambda always returning `true`) â€” accepts any certificate for any host, so the app can't detect a man-in-the-middle. Use the default (or a properly validating custom) trust manager/verifier. Suppress a vetted case with `// trust-all-ok`.
